q: What is the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics?
a: Descriptive statistics summarize and describe the data you actually have, using measures like mean, median, standard deviation, and charts. They make no claim beyond the sample. Inferential statistics use a sample to draw conclusions about a larger population you did not fully observe, quantifying uncertainty with confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and p-values. Descriptive is 'what does this data look like'; inferential is 'what can I conclude about the world from this data'.
fs: Descriptive summarizes the sample; inferential generalizes to the population and quantifies the uncertainty of doing so.
fj: They're basically the same; both just compute averages and standard deviations.

followups.q: If you have a census of the entire population, do you still need inferential statistics?
followups.a: No. If you truly measure every unit, the descriptive numbers are the parameters exactly, with no sampling uncertainty to quantify, so confidence intervals and p-values are meaningless. Inference exists precisely because you observe a sample, not the whole population. The catch is that a 'census' often still has measurement error or missingness, which can reintroduce uncertainty.

followups.q: Can a descriptive statistic mislead even when computed correctly?
followups.a: Yes. A correct mean on a bimodal or heavily skewed distribution describes a value almost no unit actually has, and a single summary hides structure like subgroups or outliers. Aggregation can even reverse trends (Simpson's paradox). Descriptive numbers are accurate but not self-interpreting, so pair them with distributions and breakdowns.

followups.q: Why is exploratory data analysis (descriptive work) usually done before any inference?
followups.a: Descriptive plots reveal the shape, outliers, and assumption violations that determine which inferential test is even valid, like whether errors look normal or variance is constant. Jumping straight to a t-test or regression risks applying a model whose assumptions the data breaks. EDA also catches data-quality bugs that would silently corrupt any downstream p-value.

q: Explain mean, median, and mode, and when you would prefer the median.
a: Mean is the arithmetic average, median is the middle value when sorted, and mode is the most frequent value. The mean is sensitive to outliers and skew, so for skewed distributions like income or latency, the median is a more robust measure of central tendency. For symmetric, outlier-free data the mean is efficient and uses all values. Mode is most useful for categorical data or to find a peak.
fs: Prefer the median for skewed or outlier-prone data because it resists distortion that pulls the mean toward the tail.
fj: Always use the mean; it's the most accurate average in every case.

followups.q: What is the breakdown point of the median versus the mean, and why does it matter?
followups.a: The median has a breakdown point of 50%: up to half the data can be corrupted to arbitrary values before the estimate is forced to blow up. The mean's breakdown point is 0%, since a single infinite outlier drags it to infinity. That is the formal reason the median is the robust choice when contamination or heavy tails are plausible.

followups.q: If the median is so robust, why not always report it instead of the mean?
followups.a: The mean is a sufficient statistic for a normal distribution and is more efficient, meaning it has lower sampling variance on clean symmetric data, so it wastes less information. The mean is also additive: total revenue is `n * mean`, but `n * median` is meaningless. For budgeting, expected value, and downstream sums you specifically need the mean.

followups.q: When is the mode the right summary and what's a pitfall of it?
followups.a: The mode is the natural center for categorical or multimodal data, like the most common device type or a peak-traffic hour, where 'average' makes no sense. The pitfall is that for continuous data the mode is undefined without binning, and the answer becomes sensitive to bin width, so a small binning change can shift the reported mode.

q: What is the difference between population and sample, and between a parameter and a statistic?
a: A population is the full set of units you care about; a sample is the subset you actually measure. A parameter is a fixed (usually unknown) numeric property of the population, like the population mean mu or proportion p. A statistic is a quantity computed from the sample, like the sample mean x-bar or sample proportion p-hat, used to estimate the parameter. Inference is the process of using statistics to estimate parameters while quantifying uncertainty.
fs: Parameters describe populations and are fixed; statistics are sample estimates of them and vary from sample to sample.
fj: The sample mean and the population mean are the same number once you have enough data.

followups.q: What is a sampling distribution and how is it different from the data distribution?
followups.a: The data distribution describes how individual observations spread out; the sampling distribution describes how a statistic like `x-bar` varies across many repeated samples. It is the bridge to inference: its spread is the standard error, and its shape (often normal by the CLT) justifies confidence intervals. They are easily confused but operate at different levels.

followups.q: Why does the population mean never appear in your formulas but the sample mean does?
followups.a: The population parameter `mu` is the fixed unknown you are trying to estimate, so by definition you cannot plug it in; you only have the sample. Estimators like `x-bar` are computed from observed data and serve as proxies. The whole point of inference is to bound how far the computable statistic likely sits from the uncomputable parameter.

followups.q: How do you precisely define the population, and why does getting it wrong matter?
followups.a: The target population is the set you want conclusions about; the sampling frame is the set you can actually draw from. When they diverge (you sample current users but want to claim things about all potential users) your statistic estimates the frame's parameter, not the target's. This coverage gap is a systematic bias that no sample size can fix.

q: What does the Central Limit Theorem state and why does it matter?
a: The Central Limit Theorem says that the sampling distribution of the sample mean approaches a normal distribution as sample size grows, regardless of the population's shape, provided observations are independent and the variance is finite. Its standard deviation, the standard error, is sigma over sqrt(n). It matters because it justifies using normal-based confidence intervals and z/t tests for means even when the raw data is not normal, as long as n is reasonably large.
fs: The CLT makes the sampling distribution of the mean approximately normal for large n, which underpins most z and t inference even on non-normal data.
fj: The CLT says that if you collect enough data, the raw data itself becomes normally distributed.

followups.q: When does the CLT fail or converge too slowly to rely on?
followups.a: It fails when variance is infinite or undefined, as with a Cauchy distribution, where the sample mean never settles. It converges slowly for heavily skewed or heavy-tailed data, so n=30 is nowhere near enough; you might need thousands. It also breaks when observations are dependent, since the effective sample size is far smaller than n.

followups.q: Why is the standard error sigma over sqrt(n) rather than sigma over n?
followups.a: Averaging n independent observations reduces variance by a factor of n (variance of the mean is `sigma^2 / n`), and standard error is the square root of that, giving `sigma / sqrt(n)`. The sqrt is why precision shows diminishing returns: cutting the error in half requires quadrupling the sample, not doubling it.

followups.q: The classic 'n >= 30' rule of thumb is often cited. Why is it unreliable?
followups.a: Thirty is adequate only for mildly non-normal data; it has no theoretical basis for skewed or heavy-tailed populations, where the sampling distribution of the mean stays skewed well past n=30. The right move is to check the actual data shape, or bootstrap the sampling distribution, rather than trust a fixed threshold.

q: Compare the normal, binomial, and Poisson distributions and when each applies.
a: The normal distribution is continuous, symmetric, and bell-shaped, defined by mean and standard deviation; it models many natural measurements and sums of effects. The binomial is discrete and counts successes in a fixed number n of independent yes/no trials each with probability p. The Poisson is discrete and models the count of rare events in a fixed interval given a constant rate lambda; it approximates the binomial when n is large and p is small.
fs: Use binomial for successes in fixed trials, Poisson for event counts per interval, and normal for continuous symmetric measures or large-n approximations.
fj: Everything can be modeled as normal, so the distribution choice doesn't really matter.

followups.q: What is special about the mean-variance relationship of the Poisson, and when does it break?
followups.a: A Poisson has variance equal to its mean lambda, a strong constraint. Real count data often shows overdispersion, where variance exceeds the mean (clustering, heterogeneity in rates), which makes Poisson standard errors too small and p-values too optimistic. The fix is a negative binomial or quasi-Poisson model that adds a dispersion parameter.

followups.q: When can you safely approximate a binomial with a normal distribution?
followups.a: When n is large and p is not near 0 or 1, roughly when both `n*p` and `n*(1-p)` exceed about 10, the binomial is bell-shaped enough for a normal approximation (with a continuity correction). Near the extremes the binomial is skewed and the normal assigns probability to impossible negative counts, so you switch to the Poisson approximation instead.

followups.q: What independence assumption do the binomial and Poisson share, and what happens when it's violated?
followups.a: Both assume events or trials are independent with a constant probability or rate. If outcomes are correlated (a server failure makes the next one likelier) the true variance inflates beyond what either model predicts, so confidence intervals built on them are too narrow. You then need a model with correlation or a dispersion term.

q: What is skewness, and how do mean and median relate under skew?
a: Skewness measures the asymmetry of a distribution. In a right-skewed (positively skewed) distribution the long tail is on the high side, and typically mean > median because large outliers pull the mean up. In a left-skewed distribution the tail is on the low side and mean < median. A symmetric distribution has skewness near zero with mean approximately equal to median. Recognizing skew guides transformations (like log) and the choice of central-tendency measure.
fs: In right skew the mean exceeds the median because the long upper tail drags the mean toward the outliers.
fj: Skewness just means the data has errors; you should delete the skewed points.

followups.q: Why does a log transform help with right-skewed data, and when is it inappropriate?
followups.a: A log compresses large values far more than small ones, pulling in the long right tail toward symmetry, which stabilizes variance and lets linear models behave. It is inappropriate when data contains zeros or negatives (log is undefined) or when the multiplicative interpretation makes no sense. For zeros people use `log1p` or a different transform like square root.

followups.q: Is the 'mean > median under right skew' rule always true?
followups.a: No, it's a reliable heuristic but not a theorem. There are constructed multimodal or discrete distributions where positive skewness coexists with mean below median. For the unimodal continuous distributions you meet in practice it almost always holds, but you shouldn't treat it as a guaranteed identity.

followups.q: How does skew affect which significance test you should use?
followups.a: Strong skew slows the CLT, so a t-test on a small skewed sample can have the wrong error rate. You either transform to symmetry first, use a nonparametric test like Mann-Whitney that ranks values, or bootstrap the statistic to get a distribution-free interval. The skew also means you should compare medians or report the effect on the transformed scale.

q: What is a p-value, precisely?
a: A p-value is the probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one you got, assuming the null hypothesis is true. It is P(data at least this extreme | H0), not P(H0 is true) and not the probability you made a mistake. A small p-value means the observed data would be unlikely under H0, providing evidence against H0. It does not measure effect size, importance, or the probability that the alternative is true.
fs: The p-value is P(data this extreme or more | H0 true); it is evidence against H0, not the probability the null is true.
fj: The p-value is the probability that the null hypothesis is true.

followups.q: Why is interpreting a p-value as P(H0 is true) called the inverse probability fallacy?
followups.a: It confuses `P(data | H0)` with `P(H0 | data)`, which Bayes' theorem says differ unless you also account for the prior probability of H0 and the alternative. Computing the actual probability the null is true requires a prior, which the p-value never uses. A p of 0.05 can correspond to a much higher false-positive probability when true effects are rare.

followups.q: If H0 is true, what distribution does the p-value follow, and why is that useful?
followups.a: Under a true null with a continuous test statistic, the p-value is uniformly distributed on [0,1]. That is exactly why a 0.05 cutoff gives a 5% Type I error rate: 5% of uniform values fall below 0.05. It also explains p-hacking, since drawing many uniform p-values makes hitting one below 0.05 by chance almost certain.

followups.q: Why is a p-value of exactly 0.049 not meaningfully different from 0.051?
followups.a: The 0.05 threshold is an arbitrary convention, and p-values are themselves noisy estimates that vary substantially across replications of the same experiment. Treating 0.049 as a discovery and 0.051 as a non-result is a false dichotomy; the underlying evidence is nearly identical. Report the effect size and interval rather than dichotomizing on a knife-edge.

q: Walk through the steps of a hypothesis test.
a: First state the null hypothesis H0 (no effect) and alternative Ha, and choose a significance level alpha (often 0.05) before seeing results. Pick an appropriate test and compute a test statistic from the data. Derive the p-value or compare the statistic to a critical value. If p < alpha, reject H0; otherwise fail to reject it. Crucially, 'fail to reject' is not 'accept H0' as true, and alpha must be fixed in advance to control the Type I error rate.
fs: Set H0, Ha, and alpha in advance, compute a test statistic, then reject H0 only when the p-value falls below alpha.
fj: Run the test, and if the result isn't significant, you've proven there is no effect.

followups.q: Why is 'fail to reject H0' not the same as 'accept H0'?
followups.a: Failing to reject means the data didn't provide enough evidence against the null, which can happen either because there's no effect or because the study was underpowered to detect one. Absence of evidence isn't evidence of absence. To actually argue for no effect you need an equivalence test that shows the effect is bounded within a negligible region.

followups.q: Why must alpha be chosen before seeing the data rather than after?
followups.a: Alpha is the long-run false-positive rate the procedure guarantees, and that guarantee only holds if the threshold is fixed independently of the result. Setting alpha after looking lets you slide it to whatever makes your observed p significant, which is a form of p-hacking that inflates the true error rate far above the nominal level.

followups.q: How do you choose the right test for your data?
followups.a: It depends on the data type and design: a t-test for comparing two means with roughly normal data, paired t-test for matched samples, chi-square for categorical counts, ANOVA for more than two groups, and nonparametric tests like Mann-Whitney when normality fails. Picking the wrong test (treating paired data as independent) violates the assumptions and invalidates the p-value.

q: When do you use a one-tailed versus a two-tailed test?
a: A two-tailed test checks for any difference in either direction (Ha: means are not equal) and splits alpha across both tails. A one-tailed test checks for a difference in a single, pre-specified direction (Ha: B > A) and puts all of alpha in one tail, giving more power for that direction but no ability to detect an effect the other way. You must choose the tail before seeing data; switching to one-tailed after a near-miss two-tailed result inflates the false positive rate.
fs: Use one-tailed only when you committed to a direction in advance; choosing the tail after seeing data inflates the Type I error rate.
fj: Always use a one-tailed test because it's easier to get a significant result.

followups.q: What is the real-world danger of a one-tailed test missing the other direction?
followups.a: If you one-tail test that a new feature increases engagement, you have zero power to detect that it actually decreased it; a harmful effect looks like 'no significant change.' In product or medical settings that blind spot can ship something damaging. Two-tailed is the safe default precisely because surprising reversals are common and costly.

followups.q: Why does a one-tailed test at alpha appear to have more power than a two-tailed at the same alpha?
followups.a: The one-tailed test puts all of alpha (0.05) in one tail instead of splitting it into two 0.025 tails, so its critical value is closer to the center and easier to exceed in the predicted direction. That extra power is real but conditional on the effect going the way you bet; it's borrowed entirely from giving up detection of the opposite direction.

followups.q: Is a one-tailed test at 0.05 equivalent to a two-tailed test at 0.10?
followups.a: In the predicted direction, yes: a two-tailed test at 0.10 uses 0.05 in each tail, matching a one-tailed test at 0.05. This equivalence is why reviewers are suspicious of one-tailed tests; they can look like a quiet doubling of the effective alpha. State and justify the directional hypothesis in advance to avoid that suspicion.

q: What is a 95% confidence interval and how do you interpret it correctly?
a: A 95% confidence interval is a range computed from the sample such that, if you repeated the sampling and interval construction many times, about 95% of those intervals would contain the true fixed parameter. The correct statement is about the long-run procedure, not about a single interval. It is wrong to say 'there is a 95% probability the true mean is in this specific interval,' because in the frequentist view the parameter is fixed and the interval is what varies.
fs: 95% refers to the procedure: 95% of intervals built this way capture the fixed parameter, not a 95% probability for one interval.
fj: There's a 95% probability that the true mean lies inside this particular interval.

followups.q: If you can't say there's a 95% chance for one interval, which framework lets you say that?
followups.a: A Bayesian credible interval does support that direct statement: given a prior and the data, there is a 95% posterior probability the parameter lies in the interval. The difference is that frequentists treat the parameter as fixed and the interval as random, while Bayesians treat the parameter as a random variable. The two often nearly coincide with weak priors, fueling the confusion.

followups.q: What does it mean for a confidence interval to have correct coverage, and how can it fail?
followups.a: Correct coverage means the long-run capture rate actually matches the nominal level, so a 95% CI captures the parameter 95% of the time. It fails when assumptions break: skewed data with small n makes a normal-based interval under-cover, and a misspecified model or ignored dependence can make the true coverage far below 95% despite the label.

followups.q: Two 95% intervals overlap slightly. Can you conclude the difference is not significant?
followups.a: No, that's a common error. Overlapping confidence intervals do not imply a non-significant difference; the correct test is a confidence interval on the difference itself, which can exclude zero even when the individual intervals overlap. The overlap heuristic is roughly equivalent to a stricter alpha, so it under-detects real differences.

q: What is the relationship between confidence intervals and hypothesis tests?
a: A two-sided hypothesis test at level alpha and a (1 - alpha) confidence interval are dual. For a test of a parameter value, you reject H0 at alpha if and only if that null value falls outside the (1 - alpha) confidence interval. So a 95% CI for a difference that excludes zero corresponds to a significant result at p < 0.05. The CI adds information the p-value lacks: the magnitude and precision of the effect, not just whether it is significant.
fs: If the null value is outside the (1 - alpha) CI, you reject at level alpha; the CI also shows effect size and precision the p-value hides.
fj: Confidence intervals and p-values are unrelated and can freely disagree.

followups.q: Why might you report a CI instead of a p-value even when they encode the same decision?
followups.a: The CI shows the effect size and its precision, so it answers 'how big and how certain' rather than just the binary 'significant or not.' A wide interval barely excluding zero signals a fragile result; a tight interval far from zero signals a solid one. The p-value collapses all of that into one number and hides whether the effect is practically meaningful.

followups.q: Does the CI-test duality hold for one-sided tests too?
followups.a: Yes, but you pair a one-sided test with a one-sided (half-open) confidence bound, not the usual two-sided interval. A one-sided test at alpha corresponds to a `1 - alpha` lower or upper confidence bound. Mixing a two-sided interval with a one-sided test breaks the exact duality and the implied error rate.

followups.q: Can a 95% CI exclude zero while the p-value is above 0.05, signaling a real disagreement?
followups.a: Not if both are computed consistently with the same method and the same alpha; by duality they must agree. Apparent disagreements come from using a different standard error, a different test (one- vs two-sided), or a CI built from a different approximation than the test. The fix is to make sure both rest on identical assumptions.

q: What factors determine the width of a confidence interval?
a: CI width is driven by three things: the variability of the data (larger standard deviation widens it), the sample size (larger n narrows it, shrinking as 1 over sqrt(n)), and the confidence level (higher confidence like 99% versus 90% widens it). The half-width for a mean is roughly the critical value times standard error, where standard error is s over sqrt(n). To halve the margin of error from sampling alone you generally need about four times the sample size.
fs: Width grows with data variability and confidence level and shrinks like 1 over sqrt(n), so quartering it needs roughly 4x the sample.
fj: You can make a confidence interval narrower just by lowering the confidence level, with no downside.

followups.q: What's the hidden cost of narrowing a CI by lowering the confidence level?
followups.a: Dropping from 95% to 90% confidence narrows the interval but raises the chance the interval misses the true parameter from 5% to 10%. You're not gaining precision, you're trading away coverage. The honest way to narrow an interval is more data or lower variance, not relabeling your tolerance for being wrong.

followups.q: Why does halving the margin of error require roughly four times the sample, not twice?
followups.a: The standard error scales as `1 / sqrt(n)`, so to cut it in half you must quadruple n, since `sqrt(4) = 2`. This square-root law is why precision gets expensive: going from a margin of 2% to 1% on a survey can mean jumping from 2,400 to 9,600 respondents.

followups.q: Why does a CI for a proportion change width depending on where p sits?
followups.a: A proportion's variance is `p*(1-p)`, which is maximized at p=0.5 and shrinks toward the extremes, so an estimate near 50% gives the widest interval and one near 0 or 1 the narrowest. That's also why power and sample-size planning often assume p=0.5 as a conservative worst case.

q: Define Type I and Type II errors and how they trade off.
a: A Type I error (false positive) is rejecting a true null hypothesis; its probability is alpha. A Type II error (false negative) is failing to reject a false null; its probability is beta. Power is 1 - beta. For a fixed sample size, lowering alpha to avoid false positives raises beta and lowers power, so the two errors trade off. You break the trade-off by increasing sample size, which can reduce both. The right balance depends on the cost of each error in context.
fs: Type I is a false positive (alpha), Type II a false negative (beta); at fixed n they trade off, and only more data reduces both.
fj: A Type I error and a Type II error are just two names for the same mistake.

followups.q: Give a setting where a Type II error is far costlier than a Type I, and how you'd respond.
followups.a: In screening for a deadly but treatable disease, a false negative (missing a real case) can be fatal while a false positive just triggers a follow-up test. There you accept a higher alpha to drive beta down, raising sensitivity. The asymmetric cost should set the thresholds, not a reflexive 0.05.

followups.q: Why does increasing sample size reduce both error types when changing alpha only trades them?
followups.a: More data shrinks the standard error, so the sampling distributions under the null and alternative pull apart and overlap less. With less overlap you can place the threshold to cut both tails of error at once. Changing alpha only slides the threshold within a fixed overlap, moving error from one side to the other.

followups.q: What is the relationship between the false positive rate (alpha) and the false discovery rate?
followups.a: Alpha is `P(reject | H0 true)`, a per-test rate conditioned on the null being true. The false discovery rate is `P(H0 true | rejected)`, the fraction of your rejections that are actually false. They differ sharply when true effects are rare: at alpha=0.05 with mostly null hypotheses, most 'significant' findings can still be false discoveries.

q: What is statistical power and what increases it?
a: Power is the probability that a test correctly rejects the null when a true effect of a given size exists; it equals 1 - beta. A common target is 80%. Power increases with larger sample size, a larger true effect size (or minimum detectable effect), lower data variance, and a higher alpha. Underpowered studies miss real effects (Type II errors) and also produce inflated, unreliable estimates of effects they do detect. You should compute the required sample size from a target power before running an experiment.
fs: Power is 1 - beta; it rises with sample size, effect size, and alpha, and falls with variance, so you size experiments to hit it in advance.
fj: Power is the same as the p-value, and you only need to check it after the experiment.

followups.q: Why do underpowered studies produce inflated effect sizes when they do find significance (the winner's curse)?
followups.a: In a low-power study, only an unusually large sampled effect can clear the significance threshold, so conditioning on 'significant' selects for overestimates. This is the type-M (magnitude) error: the published effect is biased upward, sometimes by multiples, and often fails to replicate. Power matters for the size of detected effects, not just the chance of detecting them.

followups.q: Why is a post-hoc power analysis using the observed effect size considered meaningless?
followups.a: Observed power computed from the observed effect is just a monotone transform of the p-value, so it adds no information and can't explain a non-significant result. A high p mechanically yields low observed power, which is circular. Power should be computed before the study using a meaningful target effect, not back-derived from the result.

followups.q: How does the minimum detectable effect (MDE) drive your sample-size decision?
followups.a: The MDE is the smallest effect you care to detect, and required n grows roughly as the inverse square of it, so halving the MDE roughly quadruples the sample. Setting it too small makes the study impractically large; too large risks missing a meaningful effect. You pick the MDE from business or clinical relevance, then size to detect it at your target power.

q: How would you design an A/B test from scratch?
a: Define one primary metric and a hypothesis, then choose alpha and target power (commonly 0.05 and 0.80) and a minimum detectable effect that is practically meaningful. Use those plus the baseline variance to compute the required sample size and run duration before starting. Randomize users (not requests) into control and treatment, check that randomization balances, and avoid peeking at significance repeatedly. Run for the full planned duration to cover weekly seasonality, then analyze the pre-registered metric and report the effect with a confidence interval.
fs: Pre-register metric, alpha, power, and MDE to fix sample size up front, randomize users, and run the full duration without peeking.
fj: Just launch both versions and stop the moment one of them shows a significant p-value.

followups.q: Why randomize by user rather than by request or session?
followups.a: Randomizing per request means the same user can flip between variants, contaminating their experience and breaking the independence assumption since their requests are correlated. User-level assignment gives a consistent experience and a clean unit of analysis. The trade-off is fewer independent units, which you account for in the sample-size math.

followups.q: When does the independence assumption break and the unit of randomization need to change?
followups.a: In marketplaces or social networks, treating one user can affect controls through shared inventory or social ties, so user-level randomization leaks treatment across arms (interference). The fix is cluster randomization at the level of the spillover, like by city or social cluster, so the units are actually independent. You then analyze at the cluster level.

followups.q: Why pick a single primary metric instead of evaluating many at once?
followups.a: Each metric you test is another shot at a false positive, so scanning ten metrics and celebrating any significant one inflates the family-wise error rate. A pre-registered primary metric fixes the decision rule; secondary metrics are exploratory and need multiple-comparison correction. Guardrail metrics are checked separately to ensure you didn't harm anything else.

q: What is the peeking problem in A/B testing and how do you avoid it?
a: Peeking is repeatedly checking a fixed-horizon test for significance and stopping as soon as p < 0.05. Each look is another chance for a false positive, so the true Type I error rate climbs far above the nominal alpha, often to 20% or more. Avoid it by fixing the sample size and duration in advance and analyzing only once, or by using methods designed for continuous monitoring such as sequential testing, alpha-spending boundaries, or always-valid p-values that control error under optional stopping.
fs: Peeking multiplies the chances for a false positive, so use a fixed horizon or sequential/always-valid methods that control error under continuous looks.
fj: Checking the dashboard every hour and stopping at the first significant result is fine and just saves time.

followups.q: Mechanically, why does each additional peek inflate the false positive rate?
followups.a: A fixed-horizon p-value is only uniform under the null at the planned endpoint; the running p-value wanders, and it will dip below 0.05 at some point by chance even when there's no effect. Stopping at the first dip is selecting the maximum-evidence moment from many correlated looks, so the realized error rate compounds far above 5%.

followups.q: How do alpha-spending functions let you peek legitimately?
followups.a: Group sequential designs allocate the total alpha across planned interim looks using a spending function like O'Brien-Fleming, which sets very strict early boundaries that loosen over time. Each look is judged against its own conservative threshold, so the cumulative Type I error still sums to the target alpha. You buy the ability to stop early at the cost of stricter early evidence.

followups.q: What makes always-valid p-values or confidence sequences different from fixed-horizon ones?
followups.a: Always-valid p-values and confidence sequences are constructed (often via mixture sequential probability ratio tests) to control error at every sample size simultaneously, so you can monitor continuously and stop anytime. The price is wider intervals and less power at any single fixed look compared to a one-shot test sized for that exact n.

q: What does it mean to interpret a regression coefficient in linear regression?
a: In ordinary least squares y = b0 + b1*x1 + b2*x2 + ..., each coefficient b_i is the expected change in y for a one-unit increase in x_i while holding all other predictors fixed. The intercept b0 is the expected y when all predictors are zero. Coefficients are partial effects given the model, are sensitive to which other variables are included (omitted-variable bias), and describe association, not causation, unless the design supports a causal claim.
fs: A coefficient is the expected change in y per unit of that predictor holding the others fixed, an association that shifts if you add or drop covariates.
fj: A regression coefficient directly proves that the predictor causes the outcome.

followups.q: What does 'holding all other predictors fixed' break down to when predictors are correlated?
followups.a: If two predictors move together in the data, 'holding one fixed while changing the other' describes a region with little or no actual data, so the coefficient is an extrapolation the data barely supports. That's why collinear predictors get unstable, sign-flipping coefficients: the model can't isolate effects that never vary independently.

followups.q: How does omitted-variable bias distort a coefficient, and which direction does it go?
followups.a: Leaving out a confounder that affects both a predictor and the outcome loads its effect onto the included predictor. The bias direction is the sign of the omitted variable's effect on y times its correlation with the included predictor, so it can inflate, shrink, or even reverse a coefficient. Adding the confounder is the fix, if you can measure it.

followups.q: How do you interpret a coefficient when the outcome is log-transformed?
followups.a: With `log(y)` as the outcome, a coefficient `b` means a one-unit increase in the predictor multiplies y by `exp(b)`, or approximately a `100*b` percent change for small b. So 0.05 is roughly a 5% increase per unit. This multiplicative reading is why log models suit data where effects are proportional rather than additive.

q: What is R-squared and what are its limitations?
a: R-squared is the proportion of variance in the outcome explained by the model, ranging from 0 to 1. It never decreases when you add predictors, even useless ones, so it cannot be used to compare models of different complexity; adjusted R-squared penalizes extra predictors to address this. A high R-squared does not imply the model is correct, unbiased, or causal, and a low R-squared can still come with a real, useful effect. Always pair it with residual diagnostics and out-of-sample performance.
fs: R-squared is variance explained but rises with any added predictor, so use adjusted R-squared and out-of-sample checks, not R-squared alone, to judge a model.
fj: A high R-squared means the model is correct and the relationship is causal.

followups.q: Why does R-squared mechanically never decrease when you add a predictor?
followups.a: OLS minimizes residual sum of squares, and adding a column can only give the optimizer more freedom, so the best fit can't get worse, even if the new predictor is pure noise. Its coefficient just absorbs a little of the random variation. Adjusted R-squared counters this by subtracting a penalty for each parameter relative to the residual degrees of freedom.

followups.q: Can a model have a low R-squared yet still be useful?
followups.a: Yes. In noisy domains like individual human behavior or financial returns, a model that explains only 5% of variance can still carry a real, exploitable effect, a small edge over many trades or users matters. R-squared measures how much variance is explained, not whether the estimated effect is real, precise, or actionable.

followups.q: Why is in-sample R-squared a poor measure of predictive performance?
followups.a: In-sample R-squared rewards fitting the training data, including its noise, so a flexible model can post a high value while generalizing terribly (overfitting). What matters for prediction is performance on held-out data, so you report cross-validated R-squared or test-set error. A large gap between train and test R-squared is the overfitting signal.

q: What are the key assumptions of linear regression?
a: OLS inference relies on: linearity (the mean of y is linear in the predictors), independence of errors, homoscedasticity (constant error variance), and approximately normal errors for valid small-sample tests and intervals. It also assumes no severe multicollinearity among predictors so coefficients are identifiable. Violations have specific fixes: transform variables or add terms for nonlinearity, use robust or clustered standard errors for heteroscedasticity or correlation, and check residual plots and VIF to diagnose problems.
fs: Linearity, independent and homoscedastic, roughly normal errors, and no severe multicollinearity; check residual plots and VIF, then use robust SEs when violated.
fj: Linear regression has no assumptions; you can always trust the coefficients and p-values.

followups.q: Which assumption is needed for unbiased coefficients versus only for valid inference?
followups.a: Linearity and exogeneity (errors uncorrelated with predictors) are what make the coefficient estimates unbiased. Homoscedasticity and normality of errors are not needed for unbiasedness; they only affect the validity of standard errors, p-values, and intervals. So you can have correct point estimates with wrong uncertainty, which robust SEs fix.

followups.q: Why does the normality assumption matter less as sample size grows?
followups.a: For large samples the CLT makes the sampling distribution of the coefficients approximately normal even when the individual errors are not, so t-tests and intervals stay valid. Normality of errors mainly matters in small samples where that asymptotic rescue hasn't kicked in. Influential outliers, however, remain a problem at any sample size.

followups.q: How does violating the independence-of-errors assumption show up, and why is it dangerous?
followups.a: Correlated errors arise with time series, clustered, or repeated-measures data, where observations share unmodeled structure. The danger is that the effective sample size is smaller than n, so standard errors are too small and you over-reject, declaring false significance. Fixes include clustered standard errors, mixed models, or explicit time-series error structures.

q: Explain the difference between correlation and causation.
a: Correlation measures how two variables move together, but it does not establish that one causes the other. A correlation can arise from X causing Y, Y causing X, a confounder Z driving both, selection bias, or pure chance in a small sample. To support causation you need more than association: a randomized experiment, or observational methods like controlling for confounders, instrumental variables, difference-in-differences, or natural experiments, plus a plausible mechanism. Randomization is the cleanest way to rule out confounding.
fs: Correlation can come from confounding, reverse causation, or chance, so causal claims need randomization or careful identification, not just a strong correlation.
fj: If two variables are strongly correlated, one must be causing the other.

followups.q: Why exactly does randomization let you claim causation when observational adjustment doesn't?
followups.a: Randomly assigning treatment makes it statistically independent of every confounder, observed and unobserved, in expectation, so any outcome difference can only be due to the treatment. Observational adjustment can only balance the confounders you measured and modeled correctly; an unmeasured one still biases the estimate. That coverage of unobservables is randomization's unique power.

followups.q: How can a confounder produce a correlation with no causal link at all between X and Y?
followups.a: If Z causes both X and Y, then X and Y move together purely because they share the driver Z, with no arrow between them. Ice cream sales and drownings correlate because summer heat drives both. Conditioning on Z (stratifying or adjusting) makes the spurious X-Y association vanish, which is the diagnostic.

followups.q: Can controlling for the wrong variable actually create bias instead of removing it?
followups.a: Yes. Conditioning on a collider, a variable caused by both X and Y, opens a spurious association that wasn't there, and adjusting for a mediator on the causal path removes part of the real effect you want. So 'control for everything' is wrong; you control for confounders, not colliders or mediators, which requires a causal diagram to decide.

q: What is a confounding variable and how do you handle it?
a: A confounder is a variable associated with both the treatment and the outcome that creates a spurious or distorted association between them. For example, age may drive both exercise and heart health, confounding their relationship. You handle confounders by randomizing treatment (which balances all confounders in expectation), or in observational data by stratifying, including the confounder as a covariate, matching, or using propensity scores. Failing to adjust for a confounder produces omitted-variable bias.
fs: A confounder affects both treatment and outcome and biases their association; randomize, or adjust via stratification, covariates, matching, or propensity scores.
fj: If you find a relationship in the data, confounders don't matter as long as the sample is large.

followups.q: What is a propensity score and what problem does it solve over matching on every covariate?
followups.a: A propensity score is the modeled probability of receiving treatment given the covariates, collapsing many confounders into one number. Matching or weighting on it balances all those covariates without needing exact matches in high dimensions, where exact matching fails from sparsity. Its limit is that it only balances observed confounders, never unobserved ones.

followups.q: Why can't you fix confounding by simply collecting a larger sample?
followups.a: Confounding is a systematic bias baked into how treatment and outcome relate, not random noise, so a bigger sample just estimates the biased association more precisely. You converge confidently on the wrong number. Removing the bias requires design (randomization) or adjustment for the confounder, not more rows.

followups.q: What is the difference between a confounder and a mediator, and why must you not adjust for a mediator?
followups.a: A confounder causes both treatment and outcome and biases their link, so you adjust for it. A mediator sits on the causal path (treatment causes mediator causes outcome) and carries part of the effect; adjusting for it removes the very effect you're measuring, understating the total causal impact. Distinguishing them needs domain knowledge or a causal diagram, not the data alone.

q: Explain the bias-variance tradeoff.
a: Expected prediction error decomposes into bias, variance, and irreducible noise. Bias is error from a model too simple to capture the true pattern, causing underfitting and high error on both train and test sets. Variance is error from a model so flexible it fits noise, causing overfitting that does well on training data but poorly on new data. Increasing complexity lowers bias but raises variance, so the goal is the sweet spot that minimizes total error, found via regularization, more data, and cross-validation.
fs: Error splits into bias (underfitting), variance (overfitting), and noise; tune complexity, regularization, and data to minimize their sum on unseen data.
fj: A good model just needs low bias; variance and overfitting aren't real concerns.

followups.q: How does adding more training data shift the bias-variance balance?
followups.a: More data reduces variance, since the model averages over more examples and is less swayed by any noise, but it does nothing for bias, which is a property of the model class. So more data lets you safely use a more flexible (lower-bias) model. If error plateaus high on a learning curve, you're bias-limited and need a richer model, not more data.

followups.q: How does regularization fit into the tradeoff?
followups.a: Regularization (ridge, lasso, dropout) constrains the model's flexibility, deliberately adding a little bias to cut variance, which lowers total error when the unregularized model overfits. The penalty strength is the knob: too much under-fits, too little over-fits, so you tune it by cross-validation. It's a continuous way to dial complexity rather than choosing discrete model sizes.

followups.q: Does the classic tradeoff fully explain why huge overparameterized neural nets generalize?
followups.a: Not entirely. The classic U-shape predicts that more parameters than data should overfit, yet very large models often improve again past the interpolation point, called double descent. Implicit regularization from the optimizer and the geometry of overparameterized solutions push beyond the simple picture, which is best treated as useful but incomplete intuition.

q: Why is random sampling important, and what is selection bias?
a: Random sampling gives every unit in the population a known, nonzero chance of selection, so the sample is representative in expectation and inferences generalize to the population. Selection bias occurs when the sampling or inclusion process is correlated with the outcome, like surveying only current customers about a product, or survivorship bias from analyzing only the units that 'made it.' Biased samples produce confident but wrong conclusions that more data cannot fix, since the bias is systematic, not random noise.
fs: Random sampling makes the sample representative so inference generalizes; selection bias is systematic and more data won't remove it.
fj: As long as the sample is large, it doesn't matter how you collected it.

followups.q: Give a famous example where a huge sample still gave a badly biased estimate.
followups.a: The 1936 Literary Digest poll sampled 2.4 million people yet wrongly predicted Landon over Roosevelt, because the frame (car and phone owners) skewed wealthy and Republican and high non-response amplified it. Size couldn't rescue a biased frame. It's the canonical lesson that representativeness, not raw n, drives validity.

followups.q: How is non-response bias a form of selection bias, and how do you address it?
followups.a: If the people who decline to respond differ systematically on the outcome from those who answer, the respondents are a self-selected, unrepresentative subset. You address it by maximizing response rates, comparing respondents to known population benchmarks, and reweighting or modeling the missingness. You can't fully fix it without information about the non-responders.

followups.q: Why can't reweighting always correct selection bias?
followups.a: Reweighting fixes bias on the variables you observe and model, like adjusting demographics to match the population. It can't correct bias driven by unobserved factors correlated with the outcome, and it fails entirely if some subgroups have zero probability of selection, since you can't upweight units that never appear. The bias from unmeasured selection stays.

q: What sampling methods do you know and when would you use stratified sampling?
a: Simple random sampling draws each unit with equal probability. Systematic sampling takes every k-th unit. Cluster sampling samples whole groups (like stores or regions) to cut cost. Stratified sampling divides the population into homogeneous strata (like region or device type) and samples within each, guaranteeing representation of every subgroup and reducing variance when strata differ. Use stratification when subgroups vary a lot and you want precise overall estimates plus reliable per-subgroup estimates.
fs: Use stratified sampling when subgroups differ, since sampling within strata guarantees their representation and lowers variance versus simple random sampling.
fj: All sampling methods are equivalent; just grab whatever rows are convenient.

followups.q: Why does stratified sampling reduce variance compared to simple random sampling?
followups.a: Stratifying removes the between-stratum variability from the estimator, leaving only within-stratum variance, which is smaller when strata are internally homogeneous. Simple random sampling carries the full variance including the between-group differences. The gain is largest when strata differ a lot from each other but are tight inside.

followups.q: How does cluster sampling differ from stratified sampling, and why does it usually raise variance?
followups.a: Stratified sampling samples within every group; cluster sampling samples a few whole groups and ignores the rest, to save cost. Because units within a cluster are correlated, you get less independent information per observation, so variance rises for a given n. You trade statistical efficiency for cheaper data collection.

followups.q: When can systematic sampling go badly wrong?
followups.a: Systematic sampling fails when the sampling interval k aligns with a periodic pattern in the data. If you sample every 7th day on weekly-seasonal traffic you might always hit Mondays, badly biasing the estimate. Randomizing the start helps with phase but not with a period that matches k; then you need a different design.

q: State Bayes' theorem and explain prior, likelihood, and posterior.
a: Bayes' theorem is P(H|D) = P(D|H) * P(H) / P(D), so posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. The prior P(H) is your belief about the hypothesis or parameter before seeing data; the likelihood P(D|H) is how probable the observed data is under each hypothesis; the posterior P(H|D) is the updated belief after combining them. As more data accumulates, the likelihood dominates and the influence of a reasonable prior fades, so conclusions converge regardless of the starting prior.
fs: Posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior; with more data the likelihood dominates, so a reasonable prior washes out.
fj: Bayes' theorem just means P(A|B) equals P(B|A); priors are optional decoration.

followups.q: In the disease example, why is the posterior only ~17% despite a 99%-sensitive test?
followups.a: Because the disease is rare (1% prior), the few false positives from the 99% healthy majority swamp the true positives. Even at a 5% false-positive rate, `0.05 * 0.99` healthy cases test positive versus `0.99 * 0.01` sick cases, so most positives are false. The base rate dominates the test accuracy, the heart of base-rate neglect.

followups.q: What is a conjugate prior and why is it convenient?
followups.a: A conjugate prior, combined with a given likelihood, yields a posterior in the same family, like a Beta prior with a binomial likelihood giving a Beta posterior. That makes the update a closed-form parameter tweak instead of a hard integral, which is why Beta-binomial and Gamma-Poisson pairs are everywhere. MCMC removes the necessity, but conjugacy is still handy for intuition and speed.

followups.q: What role does the denominator P(D) play and why is it often hard to compute?
followups.a: P(D), the marginal likelihood or evidence, normalizes the posterior so it integrates to one; it's the integral of likelihood times prior over all hypotheses. In high dimensions that integral is intractable, which is why most Bayesian computation (MCMC, variational inference) works with the unnormalized `likelihood * prior` and avoids computing P(D) directly.

q: How does Bayesian inference differ from the frequentist approach?
a: Frequentists treat parameters as fixed unknown constants and quantify uncertainty through the long-run behavior of procedures, yielding p-values and confidence intervals whose probability refers to repeated sampling. Bayesians treat parameters as random variables with a prior distribution, update to a posterior with data, and make direct probability statements like 'there is a 95% probability the parameter is in this credible interval.' Bayesian methods incorporate prior knowledge and handle sequential data naturally but require choosing a prior, which can be subjective.
fs: Frequentists fix the parameter and reason over repeated samples; Bayesians put a distribution on the parameter and give direct probability statements via the posterior.
fj: Bayesian and frequentist methods always give the same answer, so the distinction is purely academic.

followups.q: Why does the Bayesian approach handle sequential data more naturally?
followups.a: Today's posterior becomes tomorrow's prior, so updating incrementally as data arrives is built into the framework and the inference is valid at every step, unaffected by how often you look. Frequentist fixed-horizon tests lose their error guarantee under repeated peeking. This is why Bayesian methods suit continuous monitoring of experiments.

followups.q: How do you defend a subjective prior, and what is a weakly informative prior?
followups.a: You defend a prior by grounding it in real prior knowledge or domain constraints, and by running a sensitivity analysis showing conclusions don't hinge on it. A weakly informative prior is deliberately broad, ruling out absurd values but letting the data dominate, which guards against the false claim of total objectivity while avoiding overconfident priors.

followups.q: When do Bayesian and frequentist answers actually coincide, and when do they sharply diverge?
followups.a: With large samples and a diffuse prior they often nearly agree, since the likelihood overwhelms the prior (Bernstein-von Mises). They diverge with small samples, strong priors, or rare events, where the prior carries real weight, and in interpretation: a credible interval is a direct probability statement while a confidence interval is a long-run frequency claim, even when the numbers match.

q: What is Simpson's paradox and how do you guard against it?
a: Simpson's paradox is when a trend that holds within every subgroup reverses or disappears once the subgroups are aggregated. A classic case: a treatment outperforms within both small-stone and large-stone groups, yet looks worse overall because the groups had very different sizes and base rates. The cause is a confounding variable plus unequal group allocation. Guard against it by examining results within meaningful subgroups, not just the aggregate, and by adjusting for confounders rather than trusting pooled numbers.
fs: Aggregation can reverse a within-group trend via a confounder, so always check subgroup results and adjust before trusting the pooled number.
fj: The overall aggregated number is always the most reliable summary, so subgroup breakdowns are noise.

followups.q: Given a Simpson's reversal, how do you decide whether the subgroup or the aggregate is correct?
followups.a: It depends on the causal structure, not the data alone. If the grouping variable is a confounder (it influences both treatment assignment and outcome), the subgroup view is correct and you should adjust for it. If it's a mediator on the causal path, conditioning on it removes real effect and the aggregate is right. You need a causal diagram to choose.

followups.q: Why is unequal group allocation necessary for the paradox to appear?
followups.a: The reversal happens because a weighted average can be dominated by the larger, lower-performing strata. If allocation were balanced across groups, the pooled rates would track the subgroup rates and no reversal could occur. The confounder must drive both which group units land in and their baseline outcome rate.

followups.q: How does randomization prevent Simpson's paradox in an experiment?
followups.a: Randomized assignment makes treatment independent of any lurking grouping variable, so the confounder can't correlate with both treatment and outcome that the paradox requires. The aggregate and subgroup trends then align in expectation. This is another angle on why randomized experiments dodge the confounding traps observational data falls into.

q: What is the difference between standard deviation and standard error?
a: Standard deviation describes the spread of individual data points around the mean within a single dataset; it stays roughly constant as you collect more data from the same population. Standard error describes the spread of a sample statistic (usually the mean) across hypothetical repeated samples and equals the standard deviation divided by sqrt(n). As sample size grows, the standard error shrinks toward zero even though the standard deviation does not. Use standard deviation to describe data variability and standard error to express the precision of an estimate.
fs: SD measures spread of the data; standard error measures precision of an estimate and shrinks like SD over sqrt(n) as n grows.
fj: Standard deviation and standard error are the same thing with different names.

followups.q: When should error bars on a chart show SD versus standard error, and what do readers misread?
followups.a: Show SD when you want to convey how spread out the individual data are; show standard error (or a CI) when you want to convey precision of the mean. Readers often misread small standard-error bars as low variability, when they may just reflect a huge n on highly variable data. Always label which one you plotted.

followups.q: Why does the standard error shrink with n while the SD stays roughly constant?
followups.a: The SD is a property of the population's spread, which more sampling estimates more accurately but doesn't change. The standard error measures uncertainty about the mean, and averaging more independent observations cancels noise, so it falls as `SD / sqrt(n)`. One describes the data; the other describes confidence in a summary of it.

followups.q: Does every statistic have a standard error, and is it always SD over sqrt(n)?
followups.a: Every estimator has a standard error, but `SD / sqrt(n)` is specifically the standard error of the mean. A median, correlation, or ratio has its own, often messier, standard-error formula, and many lack a clean closed form. That's exactly when you bootstrap to estimate the standard error empirically instead of plugging into a formula.

q: What is survivorship bias and how does it distort an analysis?
a: Survivorship bias is a form of selection bias where you analyze only the units that 'survived' some process and ignore the ones that dropped out, because the failures are missing from the data. Studying only successful startups to find traits of success misses that many failures had the same traits, so the 'lesson' is an artifact of who you can still see. The classic example is reinforcing only the parts of returning warplanes that had bullet holes, when the planes hit elsewhere are the ones that never came back. The fix is to deliberately seek out the missing population and reason about why units are absent before drawing conclusions.
fs: Survivorship bias analyzes only the units that made it and ignores the missing failures, so conclusions are an artifact of who's still visible, not a real pattern.
fj: If a trait shows up in all the successful cases, it must be what causes success.

followups.q: In the warplane example, why did Wald conclude you should armor where the bullet holes were NOT?
followups.a: The returning planes are survivors, so the places they were hit are precisely the survivable hits; the planes shot in untouched areas (engines, cockpit) didn't return to be observed. The absence of holes there marks the fatal zones. Reasoning from the missing population, not the visible one, reverses the naive conclusion.

followups.q: How does survivorship bias inflate reported mutual-fund or backtest performance?
followups.a: Funds that perform badly get closed or merged and drop out of the database, so an index of currently-surviving funds overstates average returns. Backtests on today's stock universe similarly omit delisted bankruptcies. The fix is a survivorship-bias-free dataset that includes the dead funds and delisted tickers as they existed historically.

followups.q: What general question should you ask to detect survivorship bias in any dataset?
followups.a: Ask 'who or what is missing from this data, and was their absence correlated with the outcome I'm studying?' If the process that removed units (failure, dropout, delisting, churn) relates to the variable of interest, your sample is censored and conclusions are biased. Make the selection mechanism explicit before trusting any pattern.

q: What is the difference between covariance and correlation?
a: Covariance measures whether two variables tend to move together: positive means they rise together, negative means one rises as the other falls, but its magnitude depends on the variables' units, so a covariance of 500 is not interpretable on its own. Correlation is covariance standardized by the product of the two standard deviations, giving a unitless number between -1 and 1 that captures the strength and direction of a linear relationship. Because it is scale-free, correlation is comparable across variable pairs while covariance is not. Both only capture linear association and can miss strong nonlinear relationships.
fs: Correlation is covariance scaled by both standard deviations into a unitless -1 to 1 value, so it is comparable across pairs while raw covariance is unit-dependent.
fj: Covariance and correlation are interchangeable since they always have the same sign and meaning.

followups.q: Give a relationship with strong dependence but near-zero Pearson correlation.
followups.a: Points on the parabola `y = x^2` symmetric around zero have a strong, perfect functional dependence yet a Pearson correlation near zero, because the linear trend cancels: positive and negative slopes offset. A circle of points behaves the same way. Pearson measures only the linear component, so it misses symmetric nonlinear structure entirely.

followups.q: When would you use Spearman correlation instead of Pearson?
followups.a: Spearman correlates the ranks rather than the raw values, so it captures any monotonic relationship, not just linear ones, and is robust to outliers and skew. Use it when the relationship is monotone but curved, or when heavy tails would let one extreme point dominate Pearson. The cost is that it ignores the actual magnitudes, only the ordering.

followups.q: Why is the covariance matrix more fundamental than the correlation matrix in methods like PCA?
followups.a: Covariance retains the original scales and variances, which PCA needs to find directions of maximum variance in the data's native units. Using correlation instead implicitly standardizes every variable to unit variance, which changes the answer and is appropriate only when features are on incomparable scales. The choice between them materially affects the components you get.

q: Why do we divide by n-1 instead of n when computing sample variance?
a: Dividing by n-1, known as Bessel's correction, makes the sample variance an unbiased estimator of the population variance. Using the sample mean instead of the true mean makes the squared deviations systematically too small, because the data sits closer to its own mean than to the unknown population mean. Dividing by n-1 rather than n inflates the estimate just enough to cancel that bias on average. The n-1 reflects degrees of freedom: once the sample mean is fixed, only n-1 of the deviations are free to vary. For large n the difference is negligible, but it matters for small samples.
fs: Dividing by n-1 (Bessel's correction) corrects the downward bias from using the sample mean, reflecting that only n-1 deviations are free once the mean is fixed.
fj: Dividing by n-1 is an arbitrary convention that makes no real difference to the result.

followups.q: If you actually knew the true population mean, would you still divide by n-1?
followups.a: No. The correction exists only because you estimate the mean from the same data, costing one degree of freedom. If you plug in the known population mean `mu`, the deviations aren't artificially shrunk, so you divide by n and the estimator is already unbiased. The `n-1` specifically compensates for using `x-bar`.

followups.q: Does dividing by n-1 also make the sample standard deviation unbiased?
followups.a: No, and this is a subtle point. Bessel's correction makes the variance unbiased, but the square root is a nonlinear (concave) function, so by Jensen's inequality the sample SD remains slightly biased downward even with n-1. There's a further correction factor for the SD, but it's rarely used because the bias is small for moderate n.

followups.q: How does the degrees-of-freedom logic generalize beyond the simple variance?
followups.a: Each parameter you estimate from the data before computing residuals costs one degree of freedom. In regression with p predictors plus an intercept you divide the residual sum of squares by `n - p - 1`, not n, to get an unbiased error variance. The `n-1` for a single mean is just the simplest case of this general bookkeeping.

q: When you run many hypothesis tests at once, what goes wrong and how do you correct for it?
a: Each test at alpha = 0.05 has a 5% false-positive chance, so testing 20 true nulls gives a roughly 64% chance of at least one false positive; the family-wise error rate explodes with the number of tests. The Bonferroni correction controls the family-wise error rate by testing each comparison at alpha/m, which is simple but very conservative and kills power when m is large. The Benjamini-Hochberg procedure instead controls the false discovery rate, the expected fraction of rejections that are false, by ranking p-values and using a step-up threshold; it is far more powerful for exploratory or high-dimensional work. Use Bonferroni when any false positive is costly, FDR when you can tolerate a known fraction of false discoveries.
fs: Many tests inflate the family-wise error rate; Bonferroni divides alpha by m (conservative), while Benjamini-Hochberg controls the false discovery rate with more power.
fj: You can run as many tests as you want at alpha = 0.05 without any adjustment.
